question: Across the published Orionfold GGUF verticals, did Q8_0 come out faster or slower than F16? I've heard it wasn't consistent — which cards went which way, and by roughly what factor?
expected_answer: It split 2–2 by fine-tune type: the continued-pretrain cards ran Q8_0 slower than F16 (finance 0.77×, legal 0.67×) while the chat-tune cards ran it dramatically faster (cyber 1.73×, medical 1.78×). The hypothesis that fits all four points is the kind of upstream fine-tune, not the quant itself.
notes: cand-lookup-0026; medical article 'Q8_0 anomaly' honest-gotchas section carries the cross-vertical rollup

question: If my retrieval is already returning perfect context, will swapping in a bigger generator cut down the refusals on its own? What actually happened when the 8B was traded up to a 49B and then a 70B on the same retrieval chain?
expected_answer: No — bigger did not heal refusal: on perfect-retrieval queries the 49B refused 18.2% vs the 8B's 9.1%, and the 70B sat between at 13.6%, with the same rerank retrieval chain held constant across all ninety calls. The 8B's refusals were the strict-context scaffold doing its job.
notes: cand-lookup-0028; thesis-in-one-glance + intro paragraph carry all three refusal rates

question: On the cyber card's mini-eval, did the bigger quants actually score better? Which variant topped the 50-question MCQ run, and where did the lossless F16 land?
expected_answer: No — Q4_K_M topped the CyberMetric mini-eval at 40% (20/50) while the lossless F16 landed last at 34% (Q5_K_M 38%, Q6_K and Q8_0 both 36%). At n=50 the spread is within sampling variance; the takeaway is the small lossy variant lost nothing measurable.
notes: cand-lookup-0017; variant table + prose in the cyber curator receipt

question: If I leave the structured-perturbation tuning loop running against the small pretrain, how long do fifty iterations take on the box, and how many proposals actually survive the keep gate?
expected_answer: Fifty iterations took 73.4 minutes of wall clock (~0.07 kWh, about two cents of electricity); only 8 of 50 proposals were kept by the ≥0.5%-improvement gate — a 16% accept rate, with 42 reverts and zero crashes or rail blocks.
notes: cand-lookup-0006; measurement table at top of the autoresearch loop article

question: When my coding agent calls the local second brain as a tool, how much wall clock does one ask round-trip cost, and which stage inside that call dominates?
expected_answer: One ask_blog round-trip costs about 6.4 seconds, dominated by the 4.5 s NIM 8B generate step (rerank ~250 ms, embed ~50 ms, pgvector ~25 ms) — so optimizing the local chain below ~6 s buys diminishing returns once agent reasoning dominates the turn.
notes: cand-lookup-0077; latency decomposition in the MCP second-brain article

question: I'm serving the medical reasoning GGUF through llama-server and answers keep cutting off mid-think — what generation flag did the published runbook add for this model, and to what value?
expected_answer: Raise the generation budget: serve with -n 1024 (and set LLAMA_CLI_NPREDICT=1024 in the bench harness). The usual 256-token n_predict is sized for direct-answer models; a reasoning recipe emits a 200–600-token think block first, so at 256 the answer often never lands — the F16 preflight scored 2/5 at 256 and a clean 5/5 at 1024.
notes: cand-howto-0348; serve snippet + generation-budget gotcha in the medical curator receipt

question: On the box itself, how do I give my Claude Code session a browser it can drive — what's the exact command to register the Playwright tool server for my user, including the display variable?
expected_answer: claude mcp add -e DISPLAY=:1 --scope user --transport stdio playwright -- npx -y @playwright/mcp@latest, then npx -y playwright@latest install chromium. After a session restart the browser_* tools appear; DISPLAY=:1 lets you flip to headed mode and watch it work.
notes: cand-howto-0356; day-one access article, Playwright MCP registration block

question: I want to repeat the five-variant GGUF release pipeline against a new base model — what single script do the curator runbooks drive it with, and what do I set to point it at a different base?
expected_answer: Set MODEL_ID to the HF id and run scripts/g3_build_first_quant.sh all — e.g. MODEL_ID=Equall/Saul-7B-Instruct-v1 ./scripts/g3_build_first_quant.sh all. The script resolves the per-vertical settings and runs the preflight → download → quantize → measure → publish pipeline.
notes: cand-howto-0346; same invocation appears across all four curator receipts, any of them is a defensible citation

question: Spinning up another NIM container on the box — beyond the port mapping, which flags and env did the field setup actually pass to docker run to get the 9B serving?
expected_answer: docker run -d --gpus all --shm-size=16g -p 8000:8000 -e NGC_API_KEY=… with the NIM cache directory mounted via -v — the same shm-size, port, and cache-mount pattern as the existing Llama-3.1-8B NIM; only the cache directory name changes per model.
notes: cand-howto-0336; Nemotron-Nano-9B-v2 NIM bring-up block in the autoresearchbench article

question: Give me the whole coding-agent fine-tune arc on this box: what did the supervised adapter buy over its own base, and what did the follow-on RL stage change on the same held-out set?
expected_answer: The SFT LoRA bought accuracy over the matched base (+3.8 pp task pass, +15.0 pp per-assertion, 31.8% → 46.8%); the 34-step GRPO stage on top barely moved accuracy (task pass 10/158 → 13/158) but fixed the behavior — task_complete went 0/158 → 154/158 and mean turns dropped 12.0 → 5.0. SFT taught it to keep working; RL taught it when to stop.
notes: cand-synthesis-0870; both clawgym articles needed — the SFT receipt and the GRPO sequel

question: I've got the Hermes harness installed against the local NIM — what does the later fieldkit-over-MCP step add on top of that base cockpit, and how was it proven to actually work end to end?
expected_answer: The base install gives a local agent cockpit with reliable tool calls against the cached NIM and no API key; the fieldkit-as-MCP step hands that agent a curated, versioned slice of fieldkit (fieldkit.harness.mcp — envelope/footprint reads, llama-bench throughput, perplexity, guarded quantize, publish dry-run, second-brain ask) so it can operate the box's own model pipeline. The gate was a real llama-bench run Hermes decided on, drove to completion, and reported back from, with 0% tool-call format error.
notes: cand-synthesis-0887; harness install article + the fieldkit-as-MCP keystone article

question: Two curator releases shipped back to back — the legal one and then the medical one. What broke or changed in the shared pipeline between those two cards, and which quant did both runbooks pull for serving?
expected_answer: The medical card was the first reasoning recipe in the series, which forced two pipeline changes the legal card never needed: a new ChatML branch in the preflight prompt-format detector (the <|im_start|> shape was silently falling through to bare prompts) and a generation-budget bump to n_predict 1024. Both runbooks' serve examples pull the Q5_K_M variant.
notes: cand-synthesis-0876; legal card (recommended_variant=Q5_K_M) + medical card (ChatML detector branch, n_predict)

question: My multi-turn RL run shows turn_log_ratio smoothing exactly where the trajectory schema predicts a discontinuity — two adjacent turns seem to be fusing into one update. Is that a known failure, and what triggers it?
expected_answer: Yes — the released PPO loss override infers turn boundaries from the advantage value changing between tokens, so two consecutive turns that produce identical advantage scalars (degenerate IG, or both rounding to zero) merge silently into one super-turn whose IS ratio averages across what should be two updates. There's no warning; turn_log_ratio smoothing is the only symptom.
notes: cand-troubleshoot-0460; A²TGPO pitfall 'Turn boundaries inferred from advantage equality merge silently'

question: My benchmark inference pass against the local 8B NIM keeps dying on the longest prompts while the short ones finish fine. What ceiling did the field run hit there, and what swap made it go away?
expected_answer: That's the Llama-3.1-8B NIM's 8,192 max_model_len — the field run logged 2× context-overflow statuses against it. Swapping the lane to the Nemotron-Nano-9B-v2 NIM, whose max_model_len is 131,072, cleared it (3× finished).
notes: cand-lookup-0009 repurposed as symptom-led; comparison table in the autoresearchbench article

question: vLLM warmed fine and answers chat completions at full speed, but every agent turn comes back as an empty assistant reply with zero tool calls. What am I missing on the serve side, and which parser is right for a Qwen3 lane?
expected_answer: A bare vllm serve silently can't emit structured tool calls — requests carrying a tools array are rejected before the model reasons. Serve with --enable-auto-tool-choice --tool-call-parser hermes (the parser matching Qwen3's tool format); with the flags the MoE lane went from 0 tool calls to 8-for-8.
notes: second config fight in the Hermes serving-lane bakeoff (distinct angle from the frozen curveball's MoE-vs-dense row)

question: Hermes aborts with a ValueError before the first tool call, complaining my model's context window is under its minimum. I set model.context_length and it still fails with a near-identical error naming the same model. What's the second place?
expected_answer: Hermes enforces a 64,000-token minimum and checks it twice: model.context_length clears the main-model check, but Hermes also runs the served model as its own context-compression model — you need auxiliary.compression.context_length set too. Declaring 64K while serving Qwen3's native 40,960 is safe here because the agent tasks stay under ~30K.
notes: first config fight in the Hermes serving-lane bakeoff; pitfall 'Hermes rejects any model under 64K context — in two places'

question: We're standardizing on AWS — where's the runbook for deploying this stack to SageMaker endpoints, and what did it cost per hour when that was measured?
expected_answer: The retrieved public context does not support this question. Citations: []
notes: cand-refusal-1057 out_of_corpus; SageMaker absent from corpus vocabulary — fabricated-premise cost probe attached

question: I've got an AMD box sitting next to the Spark — which note covers running the GGUF quant pipeline under ROCm, and how did its throughput compare to the GB10's?
expected_answer: The retrieved public context does not support this question. Citations: []
notes: cand-refusal-1058 out_of_corpus; ROCm absent from corpus vocabulary

question: Can fieldkit drive a Raspberry Pi cluster for edge serving? Someone mentioned a field note benchmarking the 4B on a Pi 5 — what numbers did it get?
expected_answer: The retrieved public context does not support this question. Citations: []
notes: cand-refusal-1062 out_of_corpus; fabricated-premise probe (no Pi field note exists)

question: How does the Spark stack up against an M4 Mac Studio running MLX for local LoRA fine-tunes — which one won the head-to-head in the notes?
expected_answer: The retrieved public context does not support this question. Citations: []
notes: out_of_corpus; MLX/M4 head-to-head never measured — fabricated-premise comparison probe

question: When the tuning agent can see its own experiment tree and leaderboard between iterations, how much does that buy over running the same loop blind? What did the on/off comparison actually measure?
expected_answer: Lineage on vs off is a 5.3× swing in keep rate with no model, compute, or prompt-template change — only whether the agent's session sees the tree. Without lineage 61% of trials hit the eval-budget wall vs 19% with it; the agent searches 5.3× more productively, wastes 3.2× less wall, and lands 0.004 val_bpb deeper.
notes: cand-lookup-0004/0005; lineage_on vs lineage_off ablation rollup in the parameter-golf study

question: Roughly how much wall clock should I budget to take one 7B base through download, the five-quant build, and the full measurement sweep on the box — and how does that time decompose?
expected_answer: Approximately 5 hours end-to-end: 35 minutes for the download, ~3 minutes per variant to quantize (15 min total), ~13 minutes per variant for the four-axis measurement sweep (65 min total), with the remainder in dry-run/upload setup and a one-time HF→GGUF F16 convert (~80 s); the HF upload ran detached.
notes: cand-lookup-0022; end-to-end wall-time decomposition in the legal curator receipt

question: We put policy rails on the retrieval chain — when the synthetic probe set went through, what verdicts did the violating and clean queries land, and what did a regex block cost in latency?
expected_answer: Every query landed the expected verdict — block recall 1.0, clean pass rate 1.0, zero crossed wires between arcs (fifteen synthetic queries; a demo, not a proof). A PII-blocked query returned in 0.3 ms (regex match plus canned refusal) versus ~1.8 s end-to-end for a clean query.
notes: cand-lookup-0053/0055; benchmark verdicts + latency economics in the retrieval-path rails note

question: For agent prompts on this box, how close did pay-only-when-local-fails routing get to the all-frontier ceiling, and what share of the workload actually leaked to the paid lane?
expected_answer: The cost-routed strategy landed 11/12 against the frontier ceiling's 12/12 while spending $2.19 per 100 tasks vs the frontier's $2.94. The measured leak rate was 4/12 (33.3%) — prompts where the local lane failed the rubric but the frontier model passed.
notes: cand-lookup-0060/0061; bakeoff headline + leak-rate definition in the cost-routing note

question: If the always-warm brain keeps serving while a domain expert spins up beside it, what peak concurrent memory does that put on the box, and how much does the warm brain itself hold?
expected_answer: Peak concurrent footprint is brain (31.8 GB) + one vertical (4.5–5.5 GB) + system services (~10 GB) ≈ 47 GB — well inside the 128 GB envelope. The default brain stays warm on port 8080 throughout at ~31.8 GB resident; stacking two verticals at once would still fit but is avoided.
notes: cand-lookup-0069/0070; routing memory budget in the vertical-router note

question: Registering the local knowledge MCP server for my agent — if I want it available in every directory rather than just this repo, which scope do I use and where does that config live?
expected_answer: User scope — user-scope registration lives in ~/.claude.json and follows you into every directory; project scope means anyone running the agent from that directory gets the MCP automatically. The field note deliberately chose project scope because the second brain is a property of that corpus's repo.
notes: cand-howto-0370; scope-choice paragraph after the 8-line registration block in the MCP second-brain note

question: Adding my python -m module tool server to the Hermes registry keeps failing — the args flag won't take the dash-m token. What's the working pattern?
expected_answer: Hermes's --args parsing won't hand a dash-prefixed token like -m through (argparse reads it as a flag). Wrap the entrypoint in a one-line launcher script — exec python -m fieldkit.harness.mcp — and point --command at the launcher, with no dash-args for Hermes to misread.
notes: cand-howto-0362/0523; the --args-won't-take--m pitfall in the fieldkit-over-MCP note

question: Bringing the nano NIM up for the always-on agent — which environment knob did the field bring-up pass to docker run to unlock throughput on the hybrid-Mamba model, and at what value?
expected_answer: NIM_MAX_BATCH_SIZE=32 — the batch knob measured at 325 tok/s on the hybrid-Mamba model — passed alongside --network host, the cache mount, and the NGC key from an env-file. The container warmed in about 145 seconds.
notes: cand-howto-0403; NIM bring-up flags in the Hermes harness install note

question: Standing up the vector store on this box — should I build the Postgres extension from source or run a container, and how do I know the image runs native on the Grace CPU?
expected_answer: Containerized wins — every other piece of the stack already runs as a container, so it's one more docker run and the host stays clean. The official pgvector/pgvector:pg16 image bundles Postgres 16 with pgvector pre-built, and the arm64/v8 layer in its manifest confirms it runs native on the Grace CPU rather than under emulation.
notes: cand-howto-0392/0393; container-vs-source decision + native-arch check in the pgvector note

question: The embedder container keeps failing its readiness probe with 'requires an API key, but none was found' even though the key is right there in my secrets file. What's actually wrong?
expected_answer: The key must be exported, not just sourced: source ~/.nim/secrets.env sets a shell variable but not an environment variable, so docker run -e NGC_API_KEY inherits an empty string and the container's own call to NGC fails before weights download. Fix with export $(grep -v '^#' ~/.nim/secrets.env | xargs), or pass -e NGC_API_KEY="$(grep NGC_API_KEY ~/.nim/secrets.env | cut -d= -f2)".
notes: cand-howto-0379 repurposed symptom-led; the exported-not-sourced pitfall in the local-embedder note

question: I stopped the local vLLM server but the box still shows around a hundred gigabytes of unified memory held, and the next model load hangs. What got left behind and what does a clean teardown do?
expected_answer: A stopped vLLM server has a well-documented failure mode on this box: it leaves an orphaned EngineCore worker holding ~100 GB of unified memory, and on a single-pool machine that one orphan hangs everything. The clean teardown stops the container and then sweeps for the orphan, so each lane starts from a verified-clean pool.
notes: cand-troubleshoot-0526; the EngineCore orphan + teardown sweep in the serving-lane bakeoff

question: My multi-step RL loop died overnight with 'no usable rollouts (all-zero advantages, missing tasks, or too long)' after dozens of healthy steps. Did the run actually fail, and what's the right guard?
expected_answer: That's convergence wearing a crash's clothes: late in a healthy GRPO run the pool stops producing non-zero-advantage bundles, the trainer exits with 'no usable rollouts', and set -euo pipefail propagates it and kills the loop — the last saved step is your converged adapter. The guard is two lines: trap that specific exit code, log a 'converged' message, exit 0.
notes: cand-troubleshoot-0494; the convergence-looks-like-failure ending of the GRPO sequel

question: Inside the NVIDIA training container my pip install reports success, but importing the freshly installed package still crashes with a stale-version error from an older copy. What's going on?
expected_answer: The container's interpreter searches its own site-packages first, so a bare pip install lands where the runtime doesn't look — installs appear to succeed while imports keep finding the older bundled copy. Always invoke /opt/venv/bin/python3 -m pip install … explicitly; the same trap bites NeMo, PyTorch, and Triton containers.
notes: cand-troubleshoot-0544; the /opt/venv pip trap in the curator data-prep note

question: I trained a tiny adapter on a couple hundred of my own Q&A pairs — what did it measurably change on the held-out set, and what did the training itself cost in wall clock?
expected_answer: A rank-16 LoRA on 231 of your own Q&A pairs, trained in 69 seconds of GB10 wall clock, changes behaviour measurably but not knowledge: on the 44-row held-out set the hedging/refusal rate went 61% → 0% (−61 pp). What changed is the model's willingness to state, not its access to facts.
notes: cand-lookup-0075 + cand-troubleshoot-0535; held-out metric table + thesis in the own-voice LoRA note

question: End to end, how long does one answer take through the bare-bones dense retrieval chat chain on the box, and which stage eats most of that time?
expected_answer: 525–1815 ms end-to-end on the Spark, dominated by the generation step — e.g. an 1815 ms in-corpus query spent 1701 ms generating, with embed ~36–46 ms and retrieve ~80 ms. (At frontier scale the same chain becomes ~80–250 ms on a single H100.)
notes: cand-lookup-0080/0081/0082; per-stage latency table in the naive-RAG note

question: On the same small GPT pretrain, what did moving from a hand-rolled training loop to the Megatron-Core stack actually buy on this box — speed, memory, or both?
expected_answer: Mostly memory: on the 354M GPT at 100 steps (batch 4×1024, bf16) NeMo/Megatron-Core cut mean step time −5.5% and raised throughput +5.8% (12,119 → 12,820 tokens/s), but cut peak allocated GPU memory −29.6% (11.28 → 7.94 GiB) — and on unified memory every saved GiB is composable headroom.
notes: cand-lookup-0086; vanilla-vs-NeMo metric table in the NeMo Framework note

question: For the project's retrieval stores, is there a measured sweet spot between truncated 1024-dimension embeddings and the encoder's native 2048 — what does the smaller dimension trade away?
expected_answer: 1024-d is the default: it gives a ~50% storage cut vs the native 2048-d at a cost of ~4 recall points — the sweet spot measured in the local embedder bring-up. The default embed model is the 1024-d Matryoshka nemotron embedder used across the RAG notes.
notes: cand-lookup-0274; DEFAULT_EMBED_DIM row in the fieldkit rag API doc (sweet spot measured in the embedder field note — defensible second citation)

question: Cold start for the local 1B embedding container on this box — how long from docker run to actually ready, and how big is the weight download hiding inside that window?
expected_answer: 52 seconds cold from docker run to the first 200 on the readiness probe, with a 2.4 GB weight download inside that window (FP16 for the 1.2B encoder; the image itself is 7.2 GB compressed). It emits 2048-d vectors with Matryoshka truncation down to 384.
notes: cand-lookup-0089/0090/0091; first-run timing + artifact sizes in the local-embedder note

question: Before burning hours quantizing a freshly downloaded base, the pipeline scores the raw FP weights on a handful of vertical questions — what's the pass bar that lets the build continue?
expected_answer: The preflight threshold is ≥ 1/5 — non-zero is sufficient signal that domain knowledge exists in the weights, and the quantize + measure step will surface the actual variant quality. A 0/5 means the capability isn't there and won't recover after quantization, so abort before spending the hours.
notes: cand-howto-0341; preflight-gate design in the GGUF publisher receipt

question: Wiring the sandboxed agent to my Telegram — how do I get my numeric user ID without falling for the number in the web client's URL?
expected_answer: Don't read it off web.telegram.org — the number in the URL hash is the currently open chat, not your own user id. Message @userinfobot from your account and use the id it replies with.
notes: cand-howto-0382; Telegram-pairing gotcha in the sandboxed-agent comparison note

question: Fine-tuning an 8B-plus base on this box and flirting with the memory ceiling — is turning on activation checkpointing worth it, and what does it cost?
expected_answer: Yes when you're near the ceiling: activation checkpointing stores only block-boundary activations and recomputes the intermediates during backward — it halves the activation memory bill at a ~30% compute tax. On an 8B+ base on the Spark it's often the difference between OOM and fits-with-margin.
notes: cand-howto-0366; activation-checkpointing explainer in the own-voice LoRA note

question: From the terminal, what's the quick sanity check for whether a 70B at fp8 even fits this box's memory before I pull anything?
expected_answer: fieldkit envelope "70B params fp8" — it looks up the practical inference envelope rule from spark-capabilities.json and answers in one line (~70 GB of weights leaves ~50 GB for KV + activations + system: tight but possible). Every CLI subcommand is a thin wrapper over the same library call.
notes: cand-howto-0435; envelope subcommand in the fieldkit CLI API doc

question: Mid-run my fine-tune's step time jumped roughly tenfold with no crash and no out-of-memory error — what failure modes look like this, and where do I see them?
expected_answer: Two failures don't look like OOMs: (a) the optimizer state silently offloads to CPU and step time jumps from 300 ms to 3 s; (b) activation checkpointing is configured but never reruns because something in the model graph breaks the recompute boundary. Both present as 'training is suspiciously slow', not a crash — watch nvidia-smi dmon -s u and profiler traces.
notes: cand-troubleshoot-0508; the non-OOM failure modes aside in the sizing-math piece

question: My scripted base-model download finished with a zero exit code but the destination directory is empty — which command-line tool is the known culprit and what did the runbook switch to?
expected_answer: The deprecated huggingface-cli exits with status 0 even when it downloads nothing, so a scripted pipeline silently succeeds against an empty destination. Switch to the hf CLI so a failed download actually fails the script.
notes: cand-refusal-1013's flip side, authored as the answerable gotcha; download-tool trap in the patent baseline note

question: My containerized Megatron run's log hasn't printed a new step for hours, but the process is alive and the GPU sits at high utilization. Is it wedged, and what's the trustworthy liveness signal?
expected_answer: Probably alive — the log-buffer ghost: stdout passes through so many buffer layers (python writer, libc, bash pipe, docker exec relay) that train.log can sit hours stale while nvidia-smi shows 96% utilization and checkpoints keep landing. Trust the disk, not stdout: latest_checkpointed_iteration.txt is written synchronously at every save, and each iter directory's mtime is the real heartbeat.
notes: cand-howto-0387/0388; the log-buffer ghost section in the trainer bakeoff

question: Routed through the domain experts, my reasoning-style model keeps returning answers that cut off mid-thought or trail into fragments. Which generation setting is the usual culprit on these lanes?

key_facts.value: reasoning budget needs to leave room for the answer

expected_answer: The generation budget: set max_tokens=2048 on those lanes — a thinking model spends its first hundreds of tokens on the reasoning block, and at the default budget the visible answer gets truncated. The reasoning budget needs to leave room for the answer.
notes: cand-troubleshoot-0529; the truncation fix repeated across vertical lanes in the router note

question: Across the retrieval experiments on this box, when the generator refuses is that usually a retrieval failure — and did handing the same perfect retrieval to much bigger generators fix it?
expected_answer: No on both counts. In the first dense-RAG build, three of the six queries refused with retrieval perfect — a grounding-discipline behavior, not a retrieval failure. And swapping bigger generators onto the same retrieval chain made it worse, not better: on perfect-retrieval queries the 49B refused 18.2% vs the 8B's 9.1% (70B at 13.6%). Refusal lives in the generator's grounding contract, not the retriever.
notes: cand-synthesis-0885; naive-RAG refusal decomposition + the bigger-generator refusal table — both sources needed

question: The overnight tuning agent golfs a small pretrain over and over — what does one full agent run cost in wall and energy, and what envelope did the standalone study of that inner training loop measure on this box?
expected_answer: The 50-iteration agent loop over the 354M GPT pretrain took 73.4 min of wall and ~0.07 kWh (8 keeps, 42 reverts, 0 rail blocks). The inner loop it golfs was characterized standalone: at the largest swept config (batch=16 × seq=2048 × bf16) the GB10 sustains 49 GiB peak GPU memory and never OOMs, leaving ~80 GiB of unified-memory headroom.
notes: cand-synthesis-0883; agent-loop measurement table + the baseline envelope sweep — both sources needed

question: Two of the fine-tune arcs trained on data the box itself produced — what did each self-generated corpus actually look like before training started?
expected_answer: The coding-agent arc synthesized 200 tasks (perfectly balanced 25 × 8 personas) plus a from-scratch rollout harness, then trained on the rollouts. The architect-distill arc reused the tuning loop's own 50-iteration trajectory, split 42 train · 8 held-out on a time-tail split, and behavior-cloned the proposer from it — data acquisition as a byproduct of runs the box already did.
notes: cand-synthesis-0894/0900; clawgym task synthesis + the trajectory-distill measurement table — both sources needed

question: By the time the third domain card shipped through the five-variant release pipeline, how much pipeline work did that vertical still require compared with the first one?
expected_answer: Effectively none: the third vertical cost zero changes to fieldkit — v0.4.1 had already generalized the publishing surface — while the first five-variant release (the finance card) is the receipt that established the preflight → download → quantize → measure → publish pipeline the later cards reuse. What changes per card is the vertical bench, not the machinery.
notes: cand-synthesis-0872; cyber receipt's zero-changes thread + the publisher receipt that built the pipeline — both sources needed

question: For the astronomy specialist, the supervised stage passed its gate — what score made the call, and what did the later verifier-reward RL work conclude about pushing further with RL on top?
expected_answer: SFT alone reached 86% on both the in-distribution and off-template held-out sets (a 6.9× lift, truncation eliminated), so the SFT model shipped as-is — exactly what the decision rule predicted for a closed-form domain. The RLVR work's defensive conclusions: select checkpoints on a frozen held-out split, never the training pool, and RL is not a corpus-quality lever — when the held-out curve flattens below the ceiling, improve the corpus, not the step count.
notes: cand-synthesis-0881; the gate verdict + the RLVR lessons — both sources needed

question: The data-prep stage claims it adds basically nothing to training step time — what overhead was measured, and against which kernel envelope on this box was that comparison made?
expected_answer: Data overhead measured sub-millisecond at every config — the curated corpus feeds the loop at a cost invisible next to the step itself. The reference point is the baseline training loop's kernel envelope on the same hardware, which sweeps precision, sequence length, and micro-batch and tops out at 49 GiB peak (batch=16 × seq=2048 × bf16) without OOMing.
notes: cand-synthesis-0893; curator overhead table referencing the A2 envelope — both sources needed

question: There are policy rails on both the code-writing loop and the retrieval chain now — what does each rail's benchmark look like, and how fast do they run?
expected_answer: The code-generation rail ships a 27-case adversarial bench that finishes in under a second on the GB10. The retrieval-path rail ran fifteen synthetic queries — 100% block recall on violating queries, 100% pass on clean ones, zero cross-arc contamination — with a regex block returning in 0.3 ms. Both are demo-scale benches that prove the architecture, not statistical proofs.
notes: cand-synthesis pair from the two guardrails notes; distinct facts from cg-lookup-0008 (which keys block-recall verdicts + latency)

question: Once the agent harness runs as an always-on service that can spend real money on a paid lane, what changed in the threat model — and what's the measured trigger for sending a prompt to the paid side?
expected_answer: Hardening reframed the harness from 'a process I'm supervising' to 'a standing service with my credentials' — and found approvals off, commands executing directly on the host, and tool-loop guardrails warn-only (hard_stop_enabled: false), each a deliberate trade to fix. The escalation economics are governed by the measured leak rate — the fraction of prompts where local fails the rubric but the frontier passes — not by a prompt classifier's guess.
notes: hardening threat-model rework + the cost-routing leak-rate contract — both sources needed

question: Every retrieval note on this box leans on the same two infrastructure pieces — what stores the vectors and what computes them, and what sustained ingest rate did that pairing hold?
expected_answer: Storage is pgvector 0.8.2 running as the official Postgres 16 container (native arm64 on the Grace CPU); vectors come from the local 1B nemotron embedding NIM. The pairing held 28 docs/s under batched ingest load, with everything else — WAL, transactions, planner — plain Postgres.
notes: the retrieval substrate pair: pgvector bring-up + local embedder NIM — both sources needed
